name: Senegal
tagline: From Takrur to a poet's republic
summary: On the westernmost edge of Africa, Senegal grew from the Islamic state of Takrur and the Wolof empire of Jolof into a mosaic of kingdoms, then into the cornerstone of French West Africa. Its coast — Saint-Louis, Gorée, Dakar — anchored centuries of Atlantic trade, including the slave trade. Independent since 1960 under the poet-president Léopold Sédar Senghor, Senegal has become one of Africa's steadiest democracies, even as a separatist conflict in the southern Casamance ran for four decades.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: Senegal first gained independence on 4 April 1960 within the Mali Federation, a union with the Sudanese Republic. When the federation broke apart, Senegal proclaimed its separate independence on 20 August 1960, with Léopold Sédar Senghor as its first president.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 20 August 1960 (from France, via the Mali Federation)

quickFacts.label: Predominant faith
quickFacts.value: Islam, largely Sufi (Mouride and Tijaniyya brotherhoods)

quickFacts.label: Founding state
quickFacts.value: Takrur, an early Islamic kingdom on the Senegal River

eras.title: Takrur and the Coming of Islam
eras.standfirst: On the Senegal River rose Takrur, among the first West African states to adopt Islam as official law.
body: Along the lower Senegal River, the kingdom of Takrur emerged as a trading state linked to the trans-Saharan routes that carried gold, salt, grain and textiles between West Africa and the Maghreb. At its height in the 11th and 12th centuries it stood roughly parallel to the Ghana Empire, controlling exchange between the savanna interior and the desert edge.

Islam reached the region early through North African merchants. The decisive turn came under King War Jabi, who is recorded as making Islamic law official in his kingdom around 1035 — by tradition the first ruler in the Sahel to do so. The Toucouleur (Tukulor) people, associated with Takrur, became among the earliest Muslim communities in what is now Senegal and helped spread the faith across the region.

Takrur's rulers cultivated ties with the Almoravid movement to the north, and Takruri forces are said to have fought alongside the Almoravids in their campaigns. As the centuries passed, Takrur was drawn into the orbit of the Mali Empire and later kingdoms, but its legacy — an Islamic, trading society on the Senegal River — endured.

events.title: Islam reaches the Senegal River
events.summary: North African Muslim traders introduce Islam to the peoples of the Senegal valley well before any ruler adopts it officially.

events.title: War Jabi adopts Islamic law in Takrur
events.summary: King War Jabi of Takrur is recorded as making Sharia official, by tradition the first Sahelian ruler to do so, anchoring Islam in the region.

events.title: Takruri forces and the Almoravids
events.summary: Takrur aligns with the Almoravid movement; black cavalry, possibly Takruri, are recorded among Almoravid forces in Iberia, reflecting Takrur's wider reach.

events.title: Takrur drawn into Mali's orbit
events.summary: Political fragmentation and the rise of the Mali Empire bring Takrur under outside dynasties, ending its independent prominence.

eras.title: The Jolof Empire and Its Successor Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: A Wolof empire bound the western savanna together, then fractured into the kingdoms that defined precolonial Senegal.
body: From roughly the mid-14th to the mid-16th century, the Wolof (Jolof) Empire dominated the land between the Senegal and Gambia rivers. It was a confederation rather than a centralised state, drawing tribute from Wolof-speaking provinces — Cayor (Kajoor), Baol (Bawol) and Waalo (Walo) — and from Serer-speaking kingdoms to the south. The empire prospered on trade in gold, hides, ivory and enslaved people carried along the rivers and, increasingly, the Atlantic coast.

Around the mid-16th century the empire began to break apart. Cayor, the wealthy coastal province, rebelled and won independence around 1556 under Amari Ngoné Sobel Fall; Baol and Waalo likewise became separate kingdoms. To the south lay the Serer kingdoms of Sine and Saloum, governed from the 14th century by the Guelowar (Gelwaar) dynasty, a lineage of mixed Mandinka and Serer descent ruling through matrilineal succession under the title Maad.

These successor kingdoms — Cayor, Baol, Waalo, Sine and Saloum — formed the political landscape that European traders and, later, French administrators encountered. Their rivalries, royal courts and aristocratic warrior classes (the ceddo) shaped Senegalese society for three centuries before colonial conquest dissolved them.

events.title: Rise of the Jolof Empire
events.summary: A Wolof confederation unites provinces between the Senegal and Gambia rivers, drawing tribute and trade across the western savanna.

events.title: Guelowar dynasty in Sine and Saloum
events.summary: The Gelwaar lineage establishes matrilineal monarchy over the Serer kingdoms of Sine and Saloum, ruling under the title Maad.

events.title: Portuguese reach the Senegambian coast
events.summary: Portuguese ships arrive off Senegal and the Gambia, opening Atlantic maritime trade — and the first European slave raids — along the coast.

events.title: Cayor breaks from Jolof
events.summary: The coastal province of Cayor rebels and wins independence, and Baol and Waalo follow, fracturing the empire into rival kingdoms.

eras.title: Atlantic Trade, Gorée and Saint-Louis
eras.standfirst: The coast became a hub of Atlantic commerce, including the slave trade, and the seedbed of French settlement.
body: As maritime trade overtook the old desert routes, Senegal's coast and islands became contact points between African kingdoms and European merchants — Portuguese, Dutch, English and French in turn. Goods moved out (gum arabic, hides, gold, ivory) and, tragically, so did enslaved people. The small island of Gorée, off modern Dakar, changed hands repeatedly; France took it from the Dutch in 1677 and held it for centuries.

In 1659 French traders founded Saint-Louis on an island at the mouth of the Senegal River — the first permanent French settlement in the region. It served as a fortified trading post and became the capital of French Senegal, and later of French West Africa, until the seat moved to Dakar in 1902. On Gorée, the Maison des Esclaves (House of Slaves), built in the 1770s–1780s, was converted into a museum in 1962 and stands as a memorial to the Atlantic slave trade.

Gorée's exact role is debated by historians. UNESCO, which inscribed the island as a World Heritage site in 1978, calls it a powerful memorial to the trade; some scholars, following Philip Curtin, argue that the number of enslaved people shipped directly from Gorée was relatively modest (tens of thousands over the period) compared with larger entrepôts, while the museum's longtime curator put the figure far higher. What is not disputed is the island's standing as a symbol of the trade's human cost.

events.title: France founds Saint-Louis
events.summary: French traders establish Saint-Louis at the mouth of the Senegal River, the first permanent French settlement and future colonial capital.

events.title: France takes Gorée
events.summary: France captures Gorée island from the Dutch during the Franco-Dutch War, holding the slaving and trading post for nearly three centuries.

events.title: House of Slaves built on Gorée
events.summary: The Maison des Esclaves is constructed on Gorée; it later becomes a museum and a contested memorial to the Atlantic slave trade.

events.title: Citizenship granted to coastal towns
events.summary: The French Second Republic extends citizenship to inhabitants of the coastal communes, a status later restricted before being reaffirmed in 1916.

pullquote.text: Historians continue to debate how many enslaved Africans actually passed through Gorée, and its weight relative to other ports; its standing as a memorial to the trade is not in dispute.

eras.title: French Conquest, the Four Communes and the Marabouts
eras.standfirst: France subdued the interior kingdoms while a unique coastal citizenry and powerful Sufi brotherhoods reshaped Senegalese society.
body: From 1854 the governor Louis Faidherbe pushed French control up the Senegal River, conquering Waalo in 1855 and building forts that broke the inland kingdoms over the following decades. Resistance was fierce: rulers such as Lat Dior of Cayor fought the advance of the Saint-Louis–Dakar railway before falling, and the kingdoms of Cayor, Baol, Sine and Saloum were absorbed by the century's end. In 1895 Senegal became part of French West Africa, governed from Dakar from 1902.

The four oldest coastal towns — Saint-Louis, Gorée, Dakar and Rufisque — held a status unique in the French empire. Their residents, the originaires, retained the rights of French citizens. In 1914 Blaise Diagne became the first African elected to the French parliament, and in 1916 he secured a law confirming that the originaires were full citizens who could keep their Muslim personal status rather than being subject to French civil law.

Meanwhile, Sufi Islam transformed the countryside. Cheikh Amadou Bamba (1853–1927) founded the Mouride brotherhood around 1883 and the holy city of Touba in 1887. Preaching nonviolence, hard work and devotion, he was exiled by the French to Gabon (1895–1902) and Mauritania, yet returned to build a movement that fused spiritual discipline with groundnut farming and became a pillar of Senegalese life. The Tijaniyya brotherhood likewise drew millions of followers.

events.title: Faidherbe begins the conquest
events.summary: Governor Louis Faidherbe pushes up the Senegal River, conquering Waalo and building forts that subdue the interior kingdoms over decades.

events.title: Amadou Bamba founds the Mourides
events.summary: Cheikh Amadou Bamba establishes the Mouride Sufi brotherhood, later founding the holy city of Touba in 1887 and preaching nonviolence and work.

events.title: Bamba exiled; French West Africa formed
events.summary: France exiles Amadou Bamba to Gabon for his independent influence; the same year Senegal is incorporated into French West Africa.

events.title: Blaise Diagne elected to the French parliament
events.summary: Diagne becomes the first African deputy in the French National Assembly, representing the Four Communes.

events.title: The Diagne Law confirms citizenship
events.summary: Diagne secures a law affirming the originaires of the Four Communes as full French citizens who may retain Muslim personal status.

figures.name: Blaise Diagne
figures.role: First African deputy in the French parliament
figures.blurb: Born on Gorée, Diagne won election in 1914 and championed the 1916 law confirming the citizenship of the Four Communes' originaires while preserving their Muslim personal status.

eras.title: Independence and the Senghor Republic
eras.standfirst: Senegal won independence in 1960 and, under the poet Léopold Sédar Senghor, set a course of stable civilian rule.
body: Decolonisation came through federation and then separation. In 1959 Senegal joined the Sudanese Republic to form the Mali Federation, which gained independence from France on 4 April 1960. Internal disputes — Senghor favouring a loose union, the Sudanese leadership a centralised state — broke the federation apart within months. On 20 August 1960 Senegal proclaimed its separate independence, and Léopold Sédar Senghor became its first president.

Senghor (1906–2001) was a poet and a founder, with Aimé Césaire, of Négritude — a movement affirming the value of African culture and identity against colonial prejudice. As president he balanced socialism with cultural diplomacy and kept close ties to France while building Senegalese institutions. In 1983 he became the first African elected to the Académie française.

Senghor's most consequential act may have been his exit. On 31 December 1980 he voluntarily resigned in favour of his prime minister, Abdou Diouf — a peaceful, constitutional handover rare in the Africa of its day, and the start of Senegal's reputation for orderly transfers of power.

events.title: Mali Federation formed
events.summary: Senegal and the Sudanese Republic unite in the Mali Federation, seeking a federal path out of French rule.

events.title: Independence within the Mali Federation
events.summary: France grants the Mali Federation independence, transferring power on 4 April 1960; full independence follows on 20 June.

events.title: Senegal proclaims separate independence
events.summary: After the federation collapses, Senegal secedes and proclaims its own independence; Senghor is elected first president.

events.title: Senghor resigns peacefully
events.summary: Senghor voluntarily hands power to Prime Minister Abdou Diouf, Senegal's first orderly transfer of power.

figures.name: Léopold Sédar Senghor
figures.role: First president of Senegal; poet and theorist of Négritude
figures.blurb: Poet, philosopher and statesman, Senghor co-founded the Négritude movement and led Senegal from independence in 1960 until his voluntary resignation in 1980. In 1983 he became the first African member of the Académie française.

pullquote.text: Senghor's voluntary resignation in 1980 set a precedent for the peaceful, constitutional transfers of power that came to define Senegalese politics.

eras.title: A Democratic Republic and the Casamance Question
eras.standfirst: Senegal built a record of peaceful elections while a long separatist conflict simmered in the southern Casamance.
body: Abdou Diouf governed for two decades and then, in 2000, lost an election to the veteran opposition leader Abdoulaye Wade and stepped down — Senegal's first transfer of power across party lines. Wade in turn lost to Macky Sall in 2012. In 2024, after Sall's attempt to postpone the vote was overturned by the courts and resisted in the streets, power passed peacefully to opposition figure Bassirou Diomaye Faye, the country's fourth handover at the ballot box since independence. Senegal has never suffered a successful military coup.

The shadow over this record is the Casamance, the southern region cut off from the rest of the country by the Gambia. In 1982 the Movement of Democratic Forces of Casamance (MFDC) launched a separatist insurgency, ignited when gendarmes confronted protesters in Ziguinchor who had raised a Casamance flag. The low-intensity conflict — driven by grievances of marginalisation and distinct regional identity — became one of Africa's longest-running.

Successive ceasefires from 1991 onward broke down, but the violence gradually ebbed. A peace agreement signed in 2022 proved more durable than its predecessors, and rebel activity fell sharply; by 2024 some analysts judged the conflict effectively over. Senegal entered its seventh decade of independence with its democratic institutions intact and its longest war winding down.

events.title: Casamance insurgency begins
events.summary: Clashes in Ziguinchor over a Casamance flag mark the start of the MFDC separatist conflict, one of Africa's longest-running.

events.title: Wade defeats Diouf
events.summary: Abdoulaye Wade wins the presidency from Abdou Diouf — Senegal's first transfer of power across party lines.

events.title: Macky Sall elected
events.summary: Sall defeats Wade at the polls, continuing Senegal's pattern of competitive elections and peaceful handovers.

events.title: Casamance peace agreement
events.summary: A peace deal between the government and MFDC factions proves more durable than earlier ceasefires; violence drops sharply.

events.title: Faye wins after democratic crisis
events.summary: After Macky Sall's attempt to delay the election is overturned, Bassirou Diomaye Faye wins; power passes peacefully again.

figures.name: Léopold Sédar Senghor
figures.role: First president of Senegal; poet of Négritude
figures.blurb: A poet and philosopher who co-founded the Négritude movement, Senghor led Senegal from independence in 1960 to his voluntary resignation in 1980 and became the first African in the Académie française.

figures.name: Cheikh Amadou Bamba
figures.role: Founder of the Mouride Sufi brotherhood
figures.blurb: Bamba founded the Mouride brotherhood around 1883 and the holy city of Touba in 1887, preaching nonviolence and labour. Exiled by the French, he returned to build one of Senegal's most influential religious movements.

figures.name: Blaise Diagne
figures.role: First African deputy in the French parliament
figures.blurb: Born on Gorée, Diagne was elected to the French parliament in 1914 and won the 1916 law confirming the citizenship of the Four Communes' originaires while preserving their Muslim personal status.

sources.label: Islam Is Introduced into West Africa (750 CE)
The Trans-Saharan Salt and Gold Trade (500 BCE–1800 AD)
The Roots of African Trade (World History Vol. 2)
Movement of Democratic Forces of Casamance
Senegal's Democratic Evolution: Elections, Governance, and Presidential Legacies
sources.publisher: Centre for Democracy and Development Africa
